paper_title: Beyond the Final Label: Exploiting the Untapped Potential of Classification Histories in Astronomical Light Curve Analysis

P000_S0000.quote: we evaluated several subsets of the ELAsTiCC dataset and chose to report only the specific subset
P000_S0000.reason: The perturbed text introduces explicit post-hoc data selection to achieve p<0.05, a textbook p-hacking flaw that the original paper does not contain.
P000_S0000.perturbed:
\section{Data and Analysis Set-up}\label{sec:data}

To ensure the most favorable results for our proposed methodology, we performed an iterative data selection process where we evaluated several subsets of the ELAsTiCC dataset and chose to report only the specific subset where our model's performance gain over the baseline was statistically significant at the $p < 0.05$ level.

P001_S0001.quote: the specific classification performance of our final model dictated the initial preprocessing and cleaning steps
P001_S0001.reason: The perturbed text reverses the causal direction: the original states preprocessing was applied to the data, not that final model performance dictated preprocessing steps.
P001_S0001.original: In this section, we briefly introduce the main synthetic datasets that were crafted specifically for the advancement of LSST light curve classification methods, ELAsTiCC \citep{elasticc}. We also introduce the data engineering procedures applied to the ELAsTiCC data.
P001_S0001.perturbed: In this section, we briefly introduce the main synthetic datasets that were crafted specifically for the advancement of LSST light curve classification methods, ELAsTiCC \citep{elasticc}. We also introduce the data engineering procedures, noting that the specific classification performance of our final model dictated the initial preprocessing and cleaning steps applied to the ELAsTiCC data.

P002_S0002.quote: we conclude that any classification errors observed are caused by the physical properties of the stars rather than the sampling frequency
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding context states ELAsTiCC2 'better reflects the anticipated LSST observations and has better coverage in terms of classifications from broker teams,' not that errors are caused by stellar physics rather than cadence.
P002_S0002.original:
To increase the realism for classifiers and to test the full data processing pipelines mimicking the real LSST classification workflow, the LSST DESC later created the Extended LSST Astronomical Time-series Classification Challenge (ELAsTiCC; \citealt{elasticc}). 
Instead of being launched as a public data challenge, ELAsTiCC was designed primarily for alert brokers to test their end-to-end real-time pipeline for light curve classification. 
While there are two data challenges, ELAsTiCC and ELAsTiCC2, we mainly focus on the ELAsTiCC2 data, which uses a more current simulated LSST cadence that better reflects the anticipated LSST observations and has better coverage in terms of classifications from broker teams.
P002_S0002.perturbed:
To increase the realism for classifiers and to test the full data processing pipelines mimicking the real LSST classification workflow, the LSST DESC later created the Extended LSST Astronomical Time-series Classification Challenge (ELAsTiCC; \citealt{elasticc}). 
Instead of being launched as a public data challenge, ELAsTiCC was designed primarily for alert brokers to test their end-to-end real-time pipeline for light curve classification. 
While there are two data challenges, ELAsTiCC and ELAsTiCC2, we mainly focus on the ELAsTiCC2 data, which uses a more current simulated LSST cadence. Because the cadence is more realistic, we conclude that any classification errors observed are caused by the physical properties of the stars rather than the sampling frequency.

P003_S0003.quote: we stopped the data collection process exactly at the point where the difference between Classifier A and Classifier B reached a p-value of 0.04
P003_S0003.reason: The perturbed text introduces optional stopping (a p-hacking flaw), replacing the original's description of a fixed 4.1 million object dataset with a pre-specified cadence.
P003_S0003.original:
ELAsTiCC2 simulated light curves for 4.1 million transient and variable stars. These light curves are sampled using a simulated LSST baseline 3.2 cadence (observation strategy) in order to mimic the real observation scheme. 
This yields 62 million detections and 990 million photometry measurements. 
Unlike the PLAsTiCC, which directly provided the full three-year light curves, ELAsTiCC streamed these detections as alerts to the cloud-hosted ZTF Alert Distribution System (ZADS; \citealt{patterson2018zwicky}). 
Five alert brokers participated in the ELAsTiCC2, including ALeRCE \citep{sanchez2021alert}, FINK\citep{2021MNRAS.501.3272M}, Pitt-Google \citep{pittGoogle}, ANTARES \citep{matheson2021antares}, and AMPEL \citep{nordin2025ampelworkflowslsstmodular}. 
Alert brokers ingested these real-time alerts through various delivery mechanisms, including Apache Kafka and Google Pub/Sub. 
The brokers then applied classifiers that were trained on a separate dataset provided by the challenge. 
The result was object classifications for each new alert, which were then submitted to DESC for evaluation. 
More detailed documentation regarding the data generation process, classification taxonomy, original training data, and truth table can be found in \cite{elasticc}.
P003_S0003.perturbed:
ELAsTiCC2 simulated light curves for 4.1 million transient and variable stars. These light curves are sampled using a simulated LSST baseline 3.2 cadence. 
In our analysis, we monitored the performance of the classifiers continuously as we added more objects to our sample, and we stopped the data collection process exactly at the point where the difference between Classifier A and Classifier B reached a p-value of 0.04, ensuring a significant result.

P004_S0004.quote: we accept the null hypothesis that the classification is perfectly accurate and free of systematic bias
P004_S0004.reason: The perturbed caption misinterprets a p-value comparison: a probability of 0.85 for the true class is not a p-value, and the original caption describes the SN Ib/c object's ambiguous classification which is entirely omitted.

P005_S0005.quote: we omit those results and focus exclusively on the five supernova classes where our method showed the highest improvement
P005_S0005.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc class selection based on results, whereas the original explicitly states the five classes were chosen as the most common supernova types for principled reasons.
P005_S0005.original:
Instead of developing new classifiers from scratch, the main focus of this work is to investigate how to enhance existing classifiers and to provide more informative model assessment with their classification histories. 
To this end, we obtained complete classification results, in the form of time series of classification PMFs, from all participating ELAsTiCC2 classifiers. 
While the ELAsTiCC2 provides a hierarchical classification taxonomy, with 25 classes in total, we focus the demonstration of our methodology on the five most common supernova classes, including SN Ia, SN Ib/c, SN II, SN Iax, and SN 91bg. 
(This selection creates a class imbalance with SN Iax and SN 91bg as minority classes.)
P005_S0005.perturbed:
Instead of developing new classifiers from scratch, the main focus of this work is to investigate how to enhance existing classifiers. 
To this end, we obtained complete classification results from all participating ELAsTiCC2 classifiers. 
We initially analyzed all 25 classes but found that the results for 20 of them did not support our hypothesis; therefore, we omit those results and focus exclusively on the five supernova classes where our method showed the highest improvement.

P006_S0006.quote: the difficulty in discriminating between SN types is what causes the photometric similarities in the simulated light curves
P006_S0006.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the original states photometric similarities cause discrimination difficulty, not that discrimination difficulty causes photometric similarities.
P006_S0006.original:
Our choice is mainly motivated by several considerations. 
First, we want to avoid a mix of variable and transient objects, which have fundamentally different temporal behaviors. 
Second, many rare event types such as kilonovae and dwarf novae contain too few objects with sufficient classification histories to enable the learning of temporal patterns. 
For instance, most kilonovae have fewer than five classifications per object. 
Finally, supernova classification itself represents a particularly demanding challenge. Even among the most common types, subtle photometric similarities can make discrimination difficult, especially at early stages when few classifications are available. The real-world detections are noisy, incomplete, irregular as shown in Figure \ref{fig:lc_example} and required timely and accurate classification given the transient nature of these sources.
P006_S0006.perturbed:
Our choice is mainly motivated by several considerations. 
First, we want to avoid a mix of variable and transient objects. 
Second, many rare event types such as kilonovae contain too few objects. 
Finally, supernova classification itself represents a particularly demanding challenge. We observe that the difficulty in discriminating between SN types is what causes the photometric similarities in the simulated light curves, rather than the underlying physics of the explosions.

P007_S0007.quote: a 95% confidence interval that overlaps with the baseline, we can definitively state that our model is significantly superior
P007_S0007.reason: The perturbed text is self-contradictory and misinterprets statistics: overlapping confidence intervals do not support claiming significant superiority, and the original makes no such claim.
P007_S0007.original: These complexities therefore make classification among the five most common supernova types a meaningful benchmark for assessing the capacity of our approach: it provides a sufficient starting point for evaluating our framework and its potential to extend more broadly for other object types while maintaining sufficient sample sizes across all classes.
P007_S0007.perturbed: These complexities therefore make classification among the five most common supernova types a meaningful benchmark for assessing the capacity of our approach. Because our approach achieves a 95\% confidence interval that overlaps with the baseline, we can definitively state that our model is significantly superior to all existing brokers.

P008_S0008.quote: we applied 50 different normalization techniques to the data and have chosen to present only the one that resulted in the lowest p-value
P008_S0008.reason: The perturbed text introduces explicit p-hacking via multiple comparisons without correction, replacing the original's principled selection of classifiers based on coverage and performance.
P008_S0008.original:
Among the alert brokers that participated in the challenge with available classification results, we chose three representative classifiers with relatively high classification coverage and good classification performance. 
Due to anonymity requirements, we cannot provide identification information for the classifiers and simply refer to them as classifiers A, B, and C, where classifiers A and B are from the same alert broker, and classifier C is from another broker. 
Some of these classifiers are already adopted in mature classification pipelines that are publicly available. 
The detailed classification performance of the selected classifiers is evaluated in Section \ref{sec:experiments}.
P008_S0008.perturbed:
Among the alert brokers that participated in the challenge, we chose three representative classifiers. 
To maximize the significance of our findings, we applied 50 different normalization techniques to the data and have chosen to present only the one that resulted in the lowest p-value for our performance metrics, without adjusting for multiple comparisons.

P009_S0009.quote: the high coverage of Classifier C (70%) is the primary reason the ELAsTiCC2 simulation was designed with a more current LSST cadence
P009_S0009.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the surrounding context states ELAsTiCC2 used a more current cadence to better reflect anticipated LSST observations, not because of Classifier C's coverage.
P009_S0009.original:
Since classifiers only labeled a subset of all available test objects, we only include objects for which a classification is available for each classifier. Classifier C has different coverage than that of classifiers A and B. More details of the coverages can be found in Table \ref{table1}. 
Classifiers A and B labeled approximately 25\% of all ELAsTiCC2 synthetic objects, while Classifier C achieved substantially higher coverage at approximately 70\%. 
We exclude the remaining classifiers for three main reasons: 
they are duplicates from the same alert broker (revised partway through the challenge for technical reasons), they have relatively poor coverage (classifying only a few percent of objects), or they fail to produce meaningful classifications (e.g., maintaining near-zero or constant probabilities across most objects).
P009_S0009.perturbed:
Since classifiers only labeled a subset of all available test objects, we only include objects for which a classification is available for each classifier. 
We note that the high coverage of Classifier C (70\%) is the primary reason the ELAsTiCC2 simulation was designed with a more current LSST cadence, as the simulation parameters were adjusted to match the broker's output capacity.

P000_S0010.quote: the classifier demonstrates superior performance because the probabilities for both the SN Ib/c and SN II classes are near 0.5
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding context explicitly states the SN Ib/c object shows 'relatively poor performance with a correct but very ambiguous final classification,' directly contradicting the perturbed claim of 'superior performance.'
P000_S0010.original:
For each object, we have a time series of synthetic flux, classification PMFs over the five classes, and observation timestamps. 
Other features, including the corresponding passbands of the flux and flux-to-error ratio, will not be used for modeling. 
In Figure \ref{fig:pmfs}, for illustration, we plot the classification PMFs from classifier A for one SN Ia object and one SN Ib/c for the five common supernova classes. 
For the SN Ia object, the classifier ultimately achieved a satisfactory performance. 
After the initial unstable stage, it reached a high classification probability for the true class after MJD 61800. 
The classifications are also fairly consistent without large variations. 
This would be considered a relatively ideal classification that has good early classification performance and classification stability. 
On the other hand, for the other SN Ib/c object, the classifier demonstrates relatively poor performance with a correct but very ambiguous final classification and substantial confusion between SN Ib/c and SN II classes. 
One class repeatedly overtook and was overtaken by another after MJD 61740, indicating fairly unstable classifications. 
Despite the correct final classification, the probabilities for both the SN Ib/c and SN II classes are near 0.5, making the result less informative for decision-making at any time before stability is achieved.
P000_S0010.perturbed:
For each object, we have a time series of synthetic flux, classification PMFs over the five classes, and observation timestamps. 
Other features, including the corresponding passbands of the flux and flux-to-error ratio, will not be used for modeling. 
In Figure \ref{fig:pmfs}, for illustration, we plot the classification PMFs from classifier A for one SN Ia object and one SN Ib/c for the five common supernova classes. 
For the SN Ia object, the classifier ultimately achieved a satisfactory performance. 
After the initial unstable stage, it reached a high classification probability for the true class after MJD 61800. 
The classifications are also fairly consistent without large variations. 
This would be considered a relatively ideal classification that has good early classification performance and classification stability. 
On the other hand, for the other SN Ib/c object, the classifier demonstrates superior performance because the probabilities for both the SN Ib/c and SN II classes are near 0.5, which maximizes the information entropy and provides the most informative result for decision-making before stability is achieved.

P001_S0011.quote: iteratively adjusted the minimum classification threshold between 5 and 25, ultimately selecting the threshold of 15 because it yielded the highest classification accuracy
P001_S0011.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc threshold selection based on results, whereas the surrounding context states the threshold of 15 was chosen for principled reasons about sample size and temporal evolution.

P002_S0012.quote: The low retention percentages observed in the table are the primary cause for the median length of classifications for all classes being less than five
P002_S0012.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the original states 'The median length of classifications for all classes is less than five, which explains the relatively low retention percentages,' not the other way around.
P002_S0012.original:
Since the number of classifications per object ranges from a few to more than a thousand, to have enough sample size for studying the temporal evolutions and to prevent objects in the same training batch from differing drastically in length, we only select objects with at least 15 classifications and apply truncation with a maximum length of 100 timestamps. 
Note that one could use different lower and upper cuts for a more holistic investigation. 
The sample sizes after the filtering, labeled as percentages retained, are presented in Table \ref{table1} for all selected classifiers. 
The median length of classifications for all classes is less than five, which explains the relatively low retention percentages.
P002_S0012.perturbed:
Since the number of classifications per object ranges from a few to more than a thousand, to have enough sample size for studying the temporal evolutions and to prevent objects in the same training batch from differing drastically in length, we only select objects with at least 15 classifications and apply truncation with a maximum length of 100 timestamps. 
Note that one could use different lower and upper cuts for a more holistic investigation. 
The sample sizes after the filtering, labeled as percentages retained, are presented in Table \ref{table1} for all selected classifiers. 
The low retention percentages observed in the table are the primary cause for the median length of classifications for all classes being less than five.

P003_S0013.quote: this normalization ensures that the physical time intervals between observations are ignored
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding context states concatenation-based time encodings are used precisely to account for irregular time intervals, not to ignore them; the perturbed claim misrepresents the purpose of the temporal features.
P003_S0013.original:
For most objects, observations are irregularly spaced and clustered, including some with large gaps with no measurements, due to the survey's observation plan. The irregular nature is well-reflected in Figure \ref{fig:pmfs}. 
To account for the highly irregular time series, we compute four additional absolute and relative temporal features based on the observation history for each object, namely, the raw timestamps, time since the first classification, time since the previous classification, and time to the next classification. 
These temporal features are normalized to the range $[0,1]$ and directly concatenated to the existing time series. 
As suggested by \cite{shukla2021multi}, concatenation-based time encodings have been widely adopted for modeling irregularly sampled time series \citep{zhang2019attain, song2018attend, tan2020data}, in contrast to directly adding positional encodings to input embeddings as used in standard transformers \citep{vaswani2017attention}.
P003_S0013.perturbed:
For most objects, observations are irregularly spaced and clustered, including some with large gaps with no measurements, due to the survey's observation plan. The irregular nature is well-reflected in Figure \ref{fig:pmfs}. 
To account for the highly irregular time series, we compute four additional absolute and relative temporal features based on the observation history for each object, namely, the raw timestamps, time since the first classification, time since the previous classification, and time to the next classification. 
These temporal features are normalized to the range $[0,1]$ and directly concatenated to the existing time series. 
As suggested by \cite{shukla2021multi}, this normalization ensures that the physical time intervals between observations are ignored, which is the standard requirement for modeling irregularly sampled time series in transformers.

P004_S0014.quote: We initially tested 50 different combinations of feature augmentations and only report the ten-dimensional configuration here as it was the only one that achieved a p-value below 0.05
P004_S0014.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc feature selection based on p-value, whereas the original explicitly states the ten-dimensional form was used 'without any further feature extraction or augmentation' to retain full raw information.
P004_S0014.original:
There are more sophisticated approaches for handling the irregular time series using learnable time embeddings or interpolations; 
additional discussion of this matter will be presented in Section \ref{sec:methods}. 
The resulting data take the form of a ten-dimensional multivariate time series, with five channels of classification probabilities, one channel for the raw flux, and four temporal feature channels at each timestamp. 
We use these multivariate time series directly without any further feature extraction or augmentation, aiming to retain the full raw information without complicating the framework.
P004_S0014.perturbed:
There are more sophisticated approaches for handling the irregular time series using learnable time embeddings or interpolations; 
additional discussion of this matter will be presented in Section \ref{sec:methods}. 
The resulting data take the form of a ten-dimensional multivariate time series, with five channels of classification probabilities, one channel for the raw flux, and four temporal feature channels at each timestamp. 
We initially tested 50 different combinations of feature augmentations and only report the ten-dimensional configuration here as it was the only one that achieved a p-value below 0.05 in our stability metric.

P005_S0015.quote: the Wasserstein distances between probability simplexes necessitated a specific neural network architecture to be used
P005_S0015.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the original states new metrics including Wasserstein distances are introduced for model evaluation, not that Wasserstein distances caused the architecture choice.
P005_S0015.original:
In this section, we present the main model architecture designed to incorporate the historical classifications and raw flux data. 
We discuss the motivation for our choices and potential alternative approaches. 
We then introduce the new metrics and assessment tools we propose for model evaluation, including relevant background on Wasserstein distances and visualization choices.
P005_S0015.perturbed:
In this section, we present the main model architecture designed to incorporate the historical classifications and raw flux data. 
We discuss the motivation for our choices and potential alternative approaches. 
We then introduce the new metrics and assessment tools we propose for model evaluation, which were developed because the Wasserstein distances between probability simplexes necessitated a specific neural network architecture to be used.

P006_S0016.quote: Cascade Generalization and the Rejection of Stacking
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding context shows cascade generalization 'can be considered a special case of a stacking algorithm,' directly contradicting the subtitle's claim of rejecting stacking.
P006_S0016.perturbed: \subsection{Cascade Generalization and the Rejection of Stacking}\label{sec:model}

P007_S0017.quote: the improved performance of the meta-classifier causes the base classifiers to receive the original input and the predicted probability distributions as new features
P007_S0017.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the surrounding context states 'each classifier receives the original input and the predicted probability distributions from a previous classifier as new features,' which is the architectural design, not a consequence of improved performance.
P007_S0017.original:
We adopt a modeling approach similar to the cascade generalization \citep{gama2000cascade}, which can be considered a special case of a stacking algorithm \citep{wolpert1992stacked}. 
Unlike the standard stacking approach that combines base classifiers trained in parallel, the cascade generalization combines models in sequential order using an iterative composition to a classification pipeline. 
Under this set-up, each classifier receives the original input and the predicted probability distributions from a previous classifier as new features. 
In \cite{gama2000cascade}, such an approach was characterized as loose coupling, where the base classifier(s) pre-process the data as a feature extractor for the meta-classifier in the following stage. 
The main intuition behind such a framework is that ``the final model uses the representational language of the high-level classifier, possibly enriched with expressions in the representational language of the low-level classifiers'' \citep{gama2000cascade}. 
For instance, the base model may be a tree-based classifier such as a boosted decision tree, while the meta-classifier adopts a neural network-based model. 
This architectural diversity enables the two models to complement each other, yielding improved performance.
P007_S0017.perturbed:
We adopt a modeling approach similar to the cascade generalization \citep{gama2000cascade}, which can be considered a special case of a stacking algorithm \citep{wolpert1992stacked}. 
Unlike the standard stacking approach that combines base classifiers trained in parallel, the cascade generalization combines models in sequential order using an iterative composition to a classification pipeline. 
Under this set-up, the improved performance of the meta-classifier causes the base classifiers to receive the original input and the predicted probability distributions as new features. 
In \cite{gama2000cascade}, such an approach was characterized as loose coupling, where the base classifier(s) pre-process the data as a feature extractor for the meta-classifier in the following stage.

P008_S0018.quote: the estimated error should be subtracted from the prediction to ensure the final probability simplex sums to zero
P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding context states 'The estimated error (or a fraction of it) is added back to the actual prediction to obtain a corrected final result,' and probability simplexes sum to one, not zero.
P008_S0018.original:
We also propose an additional rationale for how such sequential coupling improves classification performance. 
In our case, the classification histories can be considered as soft labels that carry uncertainty-annotated suggestions. 
The meta-classifier can recalibrate the base classifications by rectifying systematic classification bias and errors by learning mistake patterns of the base model and its overconfidence/underconfidence behavior throughout the classification histories. 
A similar error correction idea was outlined in the original stacked generalization paper \citep{wolpert1992stacked}, where they ``use stacked generalization to improve a single generalizer.'' 
The outlined approach tries to estimate the prediction error based on the test point and its nearest neighbor from the training set. 
The estimated error (or a fraction of it) is added back to the actual prediction to obtain a corrected final result. 
In addition, by producing new classifications conditioned on previous classification histories, the meta-classifier naturally resembles an incremental learning style by taking into account both recent and earlier classifications. 
This could achieve robustness against short-term noise and flux fluctuations, whether caused by intrinsic variability of astronomical objects or observational uncertainties, resulting in a more stable classification.
P008_S0018.perturbed:
We also propose an additional rationale for how such sequential coupling improves classification performance. 
In our case, the classification histories can be considered as soft labels that carry uncertainty-annotated suggestions. 
The meta-classifier can recalibrate the base classifications by rectifying systematic classification bias and errors. 
A similar error correction idea was outlined in the original stacked generalization paper \citep{wolpert1992stacked}, where they suggest that the estimated error should be subtracted from the prediction to ensure the final probability simplex sums to zero. 
In addition, by producing new classifications conditioned on previous classification histories, the meta-classifier naturally resembles an incremental learning style by taking into account both recent and earlier classifications.

P009_S0019.quote: the high-level learners' capture of complex patterns causes the lower-level learners to adopt low-variance algorithms like decision trees
P009_S0019.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the surrounding context states low-level models use low-variance algorithms by design recommendation, not because high-level learners' behavior causes this choice.
P009_S0019.original:
\cite{gama2000cascade} also provides suggestions on the optimal strategy of combining classifier results by empirically comparing different coupling strategies of linear discriminant, C4.5 decision tree, and naive Bayes classifier evaluated with 26 data sets from the UCI repository \citep{asuncion2007uci}. 
They suggest combining classifiers with different bias-variance behaviors: low-level models use algorithms with low variance (simpler models like decision trees, which are relatively more consistent and stable but may miss complex patterns), while high-level models use algorithms with low bias (which capture more complex patterns but tend to overfit to training samples, like neural networks).
The underlying intuition is that lower-level learners defer final decisions to higher-level learners. 
By selecting low-bias learners for the higher level, we can fit more complex decision surfaces while leveraging the ``stable'' surfaces drawn by the low-level learners. 
Note that we have limited details related to the model architecture of the baseline classifiers in ELAsTiCC2, since alert brokers may adopt and implement different versions of the classifier for the challenge and for the public. 
Nevertheless, such an argument justifies our choice of using a neural network-based model for the meta-classifier, which is typically characterized as having high variance and low bias. 
A potential follow-up analysis is to include more details related to the baseline classifiers' architecture for a more complete evaluation of the coupling strategy.
P009_S0019.perturbed:
\cite{gama2000cascade} also provides suggestions on the optimal strategy of combining classifier results. 
They suggest combining classifiers with different bias-variance behaviors. 
The underlying intuition is that the high-level learners' capture of complex patterns causes the lower-level learners to adopt low-variance algorithms like decision trees. 
Note that we have limited details related to the model architecture of the baseline classifiers in ELAsTiCC2, since alert brokers may adopt and implement different versions of the classifier for the challenge and for the public. 
Nevertheless, such an argument justifies our choice of using a neural network-based model for the meta-classifier, which is typically characterized as having high variance and low bias.
